claim: LLM judges show 10-25 point self-preference bias: they give their own model outputs 10-25% higher scores than equivalent outputs from other models
actionable: When using LLM judges, anonymize model identities or use multi-judge aggregation to reduce self-preference bias
source_title: LLM-as-Judge: Why Automated Evals Break and How to Fix Them

claim: Verbosity bias: adding the word 'detailed' to a prompt can cause LLM judges to increase win rates by 6-23 points without content improvement
actionable: Control for length bias in LLM evaluations by instructing judges to score on content not presentation
source_title: LLM-as-Judge: Why Automated Evals Break and How to Fix Them

claim: Rubric-induced preference drift: editing evaluation rubrics to pass benchmarks can silently shift decision boundaries, degrading real-world utility by 27.9%
actionable: When updating evaluation rubrics, test on held-out real-world data; don't rely only on benchmark improvements
source_title: LLM-as-Judge: Why Automated Evals Break and How to Fix Them

claim: Five-step evaluation reform: version judge (model+prompt+rubric), build calibration subsets, use executable checks, multi-judge aggregation, read transcripts
actionable: Follow this five-step process: freeze judge version, maintain expert-labeled calibration data, use deterministic checks, aggregate judges, qualitatively audit results
source_title: LLM-as-Judge: Why Automated Evals Break and How to Fix Them

claim: Anthropic's Corbench showed 53-point score swing just by fixing grading scripts and task issues, with zero model capability change
actionable: Before claiming model improvements, verify that evaluation infrastructure changes didn't cause the score shift
source_title: LLM-as-Judge: Why Automated Evals Break and How to Fix Them
